name: Tirumala Venkateswara Temple
description: Perched atop the sacred Tirumala hills, this is one of the most visited religious sites in the world. Dedicated to Lord Venkateswara (an avatar of Vishnu), it receives millions of pilgrims every year. The temple's gopuram (tower) is adorned with intricate gold carvings and the sanctum houses a magnificent self-manifested idol of the deity. The laddu prasadam distributed here is world-famous.

name: Harmandir Sahib (Golden Temple)
description: The holiest Gurdwara in Sikhism, the Golden Temple gleams with 750 kg of gold plating over its upper floors. Set in the middle of the sacred Amrit Sarovar (pool of nectar), it welcomes over 100,000 visitors daily regardless of religion, caste, or background. The langar (community kitchen) serves free meals to all. The night reflection of the temple in the sarovar is an unforgettable sight.

name: Vaishno Devi Temple
description: Nestled in the Trikuta mountains at an altitude of 5,200 feet, Vaishno Devi is one of the most sacred Hindu temples dedicated to Goddess Vaishnavi (a manifestation of Shakti). The 13-km trek through rugged mountain terrain is itself considered a spiritual journey. The shrine is a natural cave housing three rock formations (pindis) representing the goddess in her three aspects.

name: Somnath Temple
description: One of the twelve Jyotirlingas (sacred abodes of Shiva), Somnath has been rebuilt seven times after repeated destruction. The current temple, built in 1951, stands majestically on the sea coast near Prabhas Patan in Gujarat. The sound-and-light show narrating the temple's history is breathtaking. The 'baan stambha' (arrow pillar) on the seashore marks the point beyond which there is no land until the South Pole.

name: Kashi Vishwanath Temple
description: Situated on the western bank of the holy Ganges in Varanasi, the Kashi Vishwanath temple is the most famous of all Lord Shiva temples. The main spire is covered with approximately 820 kg of gold donated by Maharaja Ranjit Singh. The 'Jyotirlinga' here is believed to be the most potent manifestation of Shiva. The temple corridor newly built nearby creates a magnificent visual axis between the temple and the Ganga ghats.

name: Meenakshi Amman Temple
description: A masterpiece of Dravidian architecture, the Meenakshi temple is dedicated to Goddess Meenakshi (Parvati) and her consort Sundareswarar (Shiva). The temple complex spans 45 acres and features 14 magnificent gopurams (gateway towers), the tallest standing 52 metres high. Each tower is encrusted with thousands of brightly painted stucco figures of deities, demons, and animals — a breathtaking visual spectacle.

name: Brihadeeswarar Temple
description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site and a crowning achievement of Chola architecture, this 1000-year-old temple was built by Emperor Raja Raja Chola I. The vimana (tower over the sanctum) soars 66 metres — remarkable for its era. The temple houses the world's largest Shiva lingam and its shadow never falls on the ground. The frescoes inside depict vivid scenes from the Ramayana, Mahabharata, and Puranas.

name: Jagannath Temple
description: The Jagannath Temple of Puri is one of the Char Dhams — the four sacred pilgrimage sites in Hinduism. Dedicated to Lord Jagannath (a form of Vishnu), the temple is famous for its annual Rath Yatra festival when enormous wooden chariots carry the deities through the streets. The Mahaprasad (food offering) cooked here is believed to be touched by the goddess Lakshmi herself and is relished by millions of pilgrims.

name: Shirdi Sai Baba Temple
description: The Shirdi Sai Baba Temple is dedicated to the revered saint Sai Baba, who is worshipped by both Hindus and Muslims. It is among the most visited pilgrimage sites in India, attracting around 25,000 devotees on a regular day and up to 100,000 on festivals. The original 'Dwarkamai' mosque where Sai Baba lived and the Chavadi where he slept on alternate nights are also part of the temple complex.

name: Kedarnath Temple
description: Located at 3,583 metres in the Garhwal Himalayas, Kedarnath is one of the most challenging and sacred pilgrimages in India. It is one of the twelve Jyotirlingas and part of both the Char Dham and Panch Kedar circuits. The temple is built in the North Indian Nagara style with massive stone slabs and opens only for six months each year (May–November). The backdrop of the snow-capped Kedarnath peak makes it one of the most dramatic temple settings in the world.
